items.prompt_md: In the complement-lookup pattern (e.g. two-sum on an unsorted array), what should you use as the hash map **key**?
options: The value you've already passed, since later elements will ask "is my complement one of these?"
The current loop index
The target sum
The final answer pair, once found
items.answer: The value you've already passed, since later elements will ask "is my complement one of these?"
items.explanation_md: You look things up by value ("have I seen this number?"), so value must be the key. The index is what you *store*, not what you search by.

items.prompt_md: Why must you check the map for the complement **before** inserting the current element into it?
options: Otherwise an element could incorrectly be treated as its own complement (e.g. matching itself when target is double its value)
It doesn't matter — insert order relative to lookup has no effect on correctness
Inserting first makes the lookup run in O(log n) instead of O(1)
Checking after insertion would raise a KeyError
items.answer: Otherwise an element could incorrectly be treated as its own complement (e.g. matching itself when target is double its value)
items.explanation_md: If you insert `x` before checking for `target - x`, and `target - x == x`, the element would wrongly "find" itself. Checking first guarantees the match is a genuinely different, earlier element.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the one-pass hash-map complement lookup over an array of n elements?
options: O(n), since each element does one O(1) expected lookup and one O(1) expected insert
O(n²), since every element is compared against every other
O(n log n), since the map keeps entries sorted internally
O(1), since a hash map lookup is instant regardless of input size
items.answer: O(n), since each element does one O(1) expected lookup and one O(1) expected insert
items.explanation_md: One pass, one constant-expected-time map operation per element, gives linear total time.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the hash-map complement lookup, in the worst case?
options: O(n), because the map may end up holding nearly every element before a match is found
O(1), because a hash map only ever stores a fixed handful of entries
O(log n), matching the depth of the map's internal tree
O(n²), because each element is paired with every other element in the map
items.answer: O(n), because the map may end up holding nearly every element before a match is found
items.explanation_md: This is the classic time-for-space trade: you spend up to O(n) extra memory to avoid the O(n²) nested-loop scan.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the brute-force alternative — trying every pair with two nested loops?
options: O(n²)
O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n²)
items.explanation_md: For each of n elements you scan (up to) the remaining n elements looking for a partner — quadratic work that the hash map eliminates.

items.prompt_md: Why is sorting the array first a worse choice than a hash map when the problem must return original **indices**?
options: Sorting reorders the elements, so you lose the original positions needed for the answer, and it costs O(n log n) besides
Sorting is illegal on arrays that contain duplicates
A sorted array can never be searched in less than O(n) time
Hash maps cannot be built from sorted input
items.answer: Sorting reorders the elements, so you lose the original positions needed for the answer, and it costs O(n log n) besides
items.explanation_md: Sorting scrambles index-to-value mapping and is also asymptotically slower than the O(n) single pass with a hash map.

items.prompt_md: A hash map lookup is described as "O(1) expected." What does "expected" mean here?
options: It's the average-case time assuming a reasonably even hash distribution; a pathological hash/collision pattern can degrade individual operations
It means the lookup is always exactly one CPU instruction
It means the time depends on where in the array the value was originally located
It means the map must be pre-sorted for the guarantee to hold
items.answer: It's the average-case time assuming a reasonably even hash distribution; a pathological hash/collision pattern can degrade individual operations
items.explanation_md: Hash maps give amortized/expected O(1) access under normal conditions; heavy collisions are the exception, not the rule, in practice.

items.prompt_md: The array contains the same value twice, e.g. `[3, 3]` with `target = 6`. Why does the "check before insert" one-pass approach still handle this correctly?
options: By the time the second 3 is processed, the first 3 is already stored in the map, so its complement (also 3) is found
It doesn't handle this case — duplicate values always require a separate pass
The map automatically deletes duplicate keys, forcing a fallback to brute force
Because 3 + 3 = 6 is treated as a sorting problem, not a hashing one
items.answer: By the time the second 3 is processed, the first 3 is already stored in the map, so its complement (also 3) is found
items.explanation_md: Each element only needs to find a *previously seen* partner — so two equal values naturally match each other on the second occurrence.

items.prompt_md: In the rare worst case where many keys collide in the underlying hash table, what happens to a single map lookup or insert?
options: It can degrade toward O(n) for that operation, though this is not the typical case
It stays O(1) no matter what, by definition of a hash map
It becomes O(log n), same as a balanced tree
It fails and raises an error
items.answer: It can degrade toward O(n) for that operation, though this is not the typical case
items.explanation_md: Heavy collisions force scanning within a bucket; well-implemented hash maps make this rare, but it's the true worst case, distinct from the expected case.

items.prompt_md: What's the mental trigger for reaching for this pattern before writing any code?
options: The problem reduces to "have I already seen X?" — fast membership or match-by-key on an unordered collection
The array is already sorted and you need the smallest window
The problem asks for elements in a specific fixed order
The input size is guaranteed to be under 10 elements
items.answer: The problem reduces to "have I already seen X?" — fast membership or match-by-key on an unordered collection
items.explanation_md: That phrase — seen-before / membership / match-by-key — is the tell that a hash map will beat a nested-loop scan.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted list of integers and a target value. You need to determine whether any two numbers in the list add up to the target.
options: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
Sort first, then scan
Brute-force every pair with two nested loops
Slide a window across the array
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
items.explanation_md: "Unsorted, find a pair summing to a target" is the classic complement-lookup trigger — one pass with a hash map beats sorting or brute force.

items.prompt_md: You're handed a list of integers and asked: does any value appear more than once?
options: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
Brute-force every pair with two nested loops
Slide a window across the array
Reverse the array and scan from the end
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
items.explanation_md: "Have I seen this value before?" is a direct membership check — a hash set answers it in one O(n) pass.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted array, you need to find the first value that repeats as you scan left to right.
options: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
Sort first, then scan
Brute-force every pair with two nested loops
Use two pointers moving toward each other
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
items.explanation_md: Sorting would destroy the left-to-right order needed to find the *first* repeat; a hash set preserves scan order while giving O(1) membership checks.

items.prompt_md: You must determine whether two given words use exactly the same letters with exactly the same counts, just rearranged.
options: Keep a hash map counting character frequency in each word and compare the counts
Sort first, then scan
Brute-force every pair with two nested loops
Slide a window across the array
items.answer: Keep a hash map counting character frequency in each word and compare the counts
items.explanation_md: Comparing letter counts is a match-by-key problem — a frequency map is the direct O(n) tool, no sorting needed.

items.prompt_md: You have an unsorted list of transaction amounts and a target refund total. You need to know if any two amounts sum exactly to the target, quickly, without disturbing the original order.
options: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
Sort first, then scan
Brute-force every pair with two nested loops
Use recursion to try every subset
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
items.explanation_md: "Without disturbing order" plus "pair sums to target" on an unsorted list is exactly the complement-lookup trigger.

items.prompt_md: Given a list of integers, decide whether every element is unique (no value occurs twice anywhere in the list).
options: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
Brute-force every pair with two nested loops
Slide a window across the array
Use two pointers moving toward each other
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
items.explanation_md: Uniqueness checking is membership checking in disguise: insert each value into a set and watch for a value that's already there.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted array of integers and must return the *positions* of the two elements whose values add up to a given target.
options: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
Sort first, then scan
Brute-force every pair with two nested loops
Slide a window across the array
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
items.explanation_md: Needing to return original positions rules out sorting; the hash map stores value-to-index so both parts of the answer come from one pass.

items.prompt_md: As you scan through a growing stream of numbers one at a time, you need to instantly know whether the current number has appeared earlier in the stream.
options: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
Sort first, then scan
Brute-force every pair with two nested loops
Use two pointers moving toward each other
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
items.explanation_md: "Instantly know if seen before" is the membership check a hash set is built for — O(1) expected per lookup as you go.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted array of integers, determine whether it contains both a number and its negation (e.g. both 5 and -5).
options: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
Sort first, then scan
Brute-force every pair with two nested loops
Use recursion to try every subset
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
items.explanation_md: "Both x and -x present" is a complement lookup where the complement function is negation instead of subtraction — same one-pass hash-set trick.

items.prompt_md: You're given two separate, unsorted lists of integers and need to determine whether they share any common value.
options: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
Sort both lists first, then scan
Brute-force every pair with two nested loops
Slide a window across the combined array
items.answer: Keep a hash map/set of values seen so far and look up each complement/duplicate
items.explanation_md: Build a set from one list, then check membership of each element from the other — one O(1)-expected lookup per element instead of a nested scan.
